tweets.text: the world is going to change faster than people expect. we're in a really good place and working on some things that i think will be genuinely surprising
fun observation: a well-curated 400M param model trained long enough often beats lazy fine-tunes of much larger models. quality of data > quantity of params, consistently.
LLMs can't reason causally, can't plan, can't learn from a few examples the way humans do. impressive demos ≠ path to human-level AI. we need a fundamentally different approach.
AlphaFold 3's predictions for small-molecule interactions are now active in 50+ drug discovery programs at partner labs. this is what AI for science looks like at scale.
ARC-AGI-2 leaderboard update: top score is 62.4% (up from 58% last week). pure LLM approaches still plateau around 20-25%. the gap is real.
used Claude to analyze 10 years of student essays in 4 hours. finding: engagement with primary sources dropped 40% since 2018 even as grades stayed flat. AI found a signal humans missed.
the key insight for good AI agents: you don't get them by just scaling — you need RLHF specifically designed for multi-step tasks with tool use. it's a different problem than chat.
reviewed 200 enterprise AI implementations. companies that succeed: (1) start with a specific use case, (2) iterate fast, (3) keep a human in the loop. still true in 2026.
recording with @sama tomorrow. lots to cover: AGI timelines, OpenAI's direction, and whether we should be worried. DM me your questions.
capabilities are advancing rapidly but our ability to ensure alignment is not keeping pace. the big labs are moving too fast. this should concern all of us.
Perplexity hit 200M monthly active users last month. people want answers, not links. the next 10 years of search will look nothing like the last 30.

beautiful result in today's paper: a 7B model fine-tuned on synthetic data it generated itself outperforms the 70B model it distilled from. recursive self-improvement is not just a thought experiment.
running local 32B models on Apple Silicon at 20+ tok/s. hardware is finally not the bottleneck for most dev workflows. the local AI story is getting genuinely interesting.
the thing that surprised me most about the last year: agentic tasks that looked hard 18 months ago are now mostly solved. the remaining hard problems are about trust, not capability.
visited 6 Chinese AI labs last week. the talent density is extraordinary. the notion that China is 2 years behind is outdated — in inference optimization and deployment they are clearly ahead.
